items.notes: First PoE standard, now folded into the base IEEE 802.3. Fine for IP phones and basic APs; superseded by 802.3bt for higher-power devices.
Widely deployed. Powers 802.11ac/Wi-Fi 5 APs and PTZ cameras. Also rolled into the base 802.3 standard.
Uses all four pairs. Powers Wi-Fi 6/6E APs, video phones and thin clients. Preferred over Cisco UPOE for new builds.
Highest-power standard PoE. Powers Wi-Fi 6E/7 APs, displays, and laptops via PoE docks.
Maintenance revision incorporated into IEEE 802.3-2022 — the current base standard. No new power classes; clarifies bt behaviour.
Cisco's pre-standard 4-pair 60 W scheme, pre-dating 802.3bt. Still supported on Catalyst, but use 802.3bt Type 3 for interoperability on new designs.
Cisco 90 W variant; functionally aligns with 802.3bt Type 4.
Late-1990s Cisco scheme for IP phones, before 802.3af existed. Legacy gear only — do not design around it.
Linear Technology / Analog Devices high-power scheme. Not interoperable with 802.3bt negotiation — avoid for multi-vendor networks.
HDBaseT Alliance power scheme used by AV gear / displays. Not IEEE PoE.
No detection or negotiation — voltage is always applied to the pairs. Can destroy gear that isn't built for the exact voltage. Common on budget APs/cameras; never mix with standard PoE ports.
